title: St. Isaac of Nineveh (Monthly) — St. Eusebius of Samosata & St. Alban of Britain
narrative:
The Monthly Commemoration of St. Isaac of Nineveh the Syrian Mystic and Doctor of Prayer, St. Eusebius the Bishop of Samosata the Confessor-Martyr, and St. Alban the Proto-Martyr of Britain

On the twenty-second of Sene, the holy Church celebrates the monthly commemoration of the holy Father Isaac of Nineveh, Bishop of Nineveh and then monk and recluse of the monastery of Rabban Shabur, the greatest of all the Syriac mystical theologians and the man whose writings on prayer, compunction, and the love of God have exercised a broader ecumenical influence than those of almost any other figure in the Eastern Christian tradition. Isaac was a bishop for only five months before resigning his see — overcome, the tradition says, by the magnitude of the responsibility and his own sense of inadequacy — and retreating to the monastic life that suited him better, where he spent the remaining decades of his life writing the homilies and discourses that constitute the most profound systematic account of the inner life of prayer in all of Eastern Christianity. His writings were translated into Greek, Arabic, Ethiopic, and later into Latin and other languages, and their influence spread from the Syriac monastic tradition into the Byzantine and Ethiopian traditions and eventually into the Russian spiritual tradition, where they formed one of the primary sources of the Hesychast movement.

The theology of Isaac of Nineveh is a theology of the merciful heart: his most celebrated passage describes the person who has achieved the highest degree of prayer as someone in whom the heart has been enlarged until it encompasses all creatures — humans, animals, even demons — and weeps for all of them with a love that Isaac describes as the image of God's own love for his creation. This theology of universal compassion, so extreme that it extends even to the fallen angels, is the tradition's most ambitious statement of the transformative possibilities of the contemplative life: the monk who prays long enough and deeply enough does not become more exclusive in his love but more inclusive, does not retreat further from the world in his heart even as he retreats from it in his body, but finds in the depths of solitude a love that is wider than any social life could make it. Isaac is the tradition's icon of the paradox of mystical withdrawal: the hermit who loves everything.

Saint Eusebius of Samosata is a confessor-bishop of the Nicene controversy — a man who spent years in exile for his defence of the Nicene faith against the Arian emperors, who used his periods of freedom to travel secretly through the churches of Syria and Cilicia confirming them in Nicene orthodoxy, ordaining clergy, and strengthening bishops, and who was eventually martyred not by imperial decree but by a private act of violence: an Arian woman threw a tile at him from a rooftop as he passed and killed him. The manner of Eusebius's death is one of the tradition's stranger martyrdom narratives, and the tradition handles it with characteristic theological ingenuity, noting that the woman intended murder and got it, and that the mode of death — sudden, from above, by a tile rather than a sword — does not diminish the martyrdom because what matters is not the instrument of death but the reason the martyr was there to be killed: Eusebius was in the streets of that city strengthening the faithful, and he was killed for what he was doing, which was the work of God.

Saint Alban of Britain is the first martyr of the island of Britain and one of the most beloved saints of the English tradition: a Roman soldier or official at Verulamium who sheltered a fleeing Christian priest, was converted by the priest's witness during his time of hiding, dressed himself in the priest's cloak and presented himself to the soldiers who came to arrest the priest, was discovered, refused to deny his new faith, and was executed on the hill above the city that would later bear his name — Saint Albans. Alban's martyrdom is one of the tradition's great stories of the host who becomes the witness: the man who offered hospitality to a fugitive found the fugitive's God, and having found God he was willing to give up the only thing he had left in order to keep what he had found. The twenty-second day of Sene gathers around the monthly feast of the great mystic of universal compassion a bishop who kept the faith alive by night in disguise and a soldier who found the faith through a stranger's shelter. Amen.
